body_text:
Ask the publishers to restore access to 500,000+ books.
The Indictment of the major Nazi war criminals was presented to the International Military Tribunal on18th October 1945 prior to the trials which opened on 19th November 1945 in Nuremberg.
The British Parliament received the text of the Indictment in the form of a White Paper, and this copy of the original contemporary document has been uploaded to mark the 75th anniversary of its publication. It preserves “history that cannot be rewritten, facts that cannot be contorted, and events that cannot be erased”.
The 24 defendants were charged with:
· Crimes against Peace
· War Crimes
· Crimes against Humanity, and
The 50-page document describes:
· how the “Nazi conspirators” seized “totalitarian control over Germany” by “the extermination of potential internal resistance”, while the “annihilation of the Jews became an official State policy”
· plans and their execution “for the assault on Austria and Czechoslovakia”, 1936-39; for the invasion of Poland in 1939; followed by starting “a general war of aggression… and attacks on Denmark, Norway, Belgium, The Netherlands, Luxembourg, Yugoslavia, and Greece”, 1939-1941
· how, in 1941 “the “Nazi conspirators… without any declaration of war invaded Soviet territory… [and] began to carry out the destruction of cities, towns and villages… as well as the annihilation of old people, women and children, especially Belo-Russians and Ukranians”
· steps taken for “collaboration with Italy and, Japan and aggressive war against the United States”
· details of the “murder and illtreatment of civilian populations of or in Occupied Territory and on the High Seas… carried out by divers means, including shooting, hanging, gassing, starvation… kickings, beatings, brutality and torture of all kinds…” as well as “deliberate and systematic genocide, viz., the extermination of racial and national groups…”
· examples of “deportation for slave labour… of… civilian populations”; the “murder and ill-treatment of prisoners of war”; the “killing of hostages”; and many more “merciless atrocities”.
The original text will (for many) offer a more chilling account than any synopsis. It includes the first ‘official’ use of the word ‘genocide’ in a public document, while prosecution for ‘crimes against humanity’ first took place at Nuremberg.
